label: 90_syriza_tsipras_greek_greece
description: The Greek election in May 2015 resulted in the defeat of traditional parties and the rise of Syriza, leading to political instability. The failure to form a government after these elections prompted further elections in January 2015, highlighting the challenges faced by Greece's democratic process.

earliest_articles.title: Rocking the Cradle of Democracy
earliest_articles.excerpt: After three days in Athens, including a first hand look at the street violence that once again rocked the cradle of democracy, a few immediate conclusions come to mind. First, austerity is as hard as it sounds. Across the board, people here are feeling the effects of nearly three years of economic bad news. But unlike…

earliest_articles.title: Power-Sharing in Greece Would be Great News for All
earliest_articles.excerpt: Recent news suggested that the current Greek center-left Panhellenic Social Movement (PASOK) government might morph into a national unity government including the main Greek center-right New Democracy Party and possibly several technocratic ministers. Some reports say further that Prime Minister George Papandreou…

earliest_articles.title: Why Greeks are Angry, Scared and Trapped
earliest_articles.excerpt: Greeks will come up with the cash to make payments on their debt. Sustained growth will only happen when the government takes an ax to the knots of bureaucratic red tape that hamper even the simplest of business transactions. But the benefits of that kind of reform could take years to materialize. Policymakers in…

newest_articles.title: Greek elections: The first defeat of populism in Europe?
newest_articles.excerpt: The liberal leader of the center-right New Democracy party, Kyriakos Mitsotakis, was sworn in as Greece’s prime minister on July 8, after last Sunday’s snap elections in which he won 39.8 percent of the votes, while the ruling populist Syriza party secured a 31.5 percent share. The electoral system, which confers a…

newest_articles.title: The center right ousts leftists in Greece
newest_articles.excerpt: In Sunday’s Greek elections, voters handed the reins of government over to the center-right New Democracy party, ushering Prime Minister Alexis Tsipras and his leftist Syriza party out of power. While New Democracy received only about 40% of the vote, it will have a slight majority in parliament, where the party with…

newest_articles.title: Brexit versus Grexit: Why you might call a referendum and then reject its outcome
newest_articles.excerpt: , but it is imperfect, in that neither side knows exactly how much Europe would be hurt if Greece were to exit the Eurozone. This assumption seems plausible since there is no historical precedent of a country leaving the Eurozone. The unknown cost of Grexit determines the bargaining power in the bailout negotiations.…

central_articles.title: Will This Greek Tragedy Climax in the Death of the Euro?
central_articles.excerpt: Greece could well be out of the euro soon, depending on the results of the election scheduled there for June 17. Such an exit could conceivably occur even before the election if enough people take actions to anticipate a euro exit, unintentionally precipitating the event. An exit would inevitably be messy and has the…

central_articles.title: Greece's Latest Travails Are Not Europe's Problem This Time
central_articles.excerpt: The failure of Prime Minister Antonis Samaras to assemble enough votes to install a new Greek president has resulted in a call for elections for January 25. This worrisome development reflects Samaras's inability to articulate a political strategy beyond the narrow one of saving his own coalition of only 155 out of…

central_articles.title: Greece's Election: Tough Choices and a Sense of Déjà vu
central_articles.excerpt: The Greek election on May 6 delivered a result that was only marginally surprising, defeating both "establishment parties," the center-right New Democracy and the Socialists, or PASOK. Together they fell short of a majority, with only 108 seats for New Democracy and 41 for the Socialists, out of 300 parliamentary…
